text: The robotic surgical system is docked in the left lateral abdomen, revealing an umbilical hernia with non-reducible omentum visible within the hernia sac.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, employing ProGrasp forceps and surgical scissors to manipulate and separate the tissues.
Following the reduction of hernia contents, a 2x3 centimeter umbilical defect is visible.

The surgeon measures the peritoneum ipsilateral to the trocar sites, ensuring it is at least five centimeters away from the proximal edge of the umbilical defect to allow for adequate mesh overlap during the hernia repair.
The surgeon uses scissors to perform blunt dissection, carefully separating the thin peritoneum from the posterior sheath.
The surgeon performs mutual dissection to expose the fat underlying the rectus muscle, aiding in the development of the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon continues dissection, working medially before returning to the lateral side where the peritoneum appears thin, using careful manipulation to separate tissue layers.
The surgeon uses adequate retraction to identify stretch fibers and ensure adherence to the correct anatomical planes during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection both cephalad and caudad to visually identify and delineate the hernia sac before attempting its reduction.
With adequate retraction, the surgeon clearly identifies and dissects the peritoneum from the posterior sheath, revealing distinct planes of dissection.
The surgeon identifies the arcuate line on the screen, highlighting it as a crucial landmark to avoid injury to the inferior epigastric vessels during the hernia repair procedure.
An arrow on the screen indicates the precise location of the hernia sac within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects cephalad to the hernia sac, carefully separating the tissue planes above the sac to facilitate its reduction.
The surgeon begins the reduction of the hernia sac, which is partially encircled, using careful manipulation to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon incises the fibers of the transversalis fascia to achieve true preperitoneal reduction, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon distinguishes incarcerated preperitoneal fat from the transversalis fascia and reduces it along with the peritoneal sac, visibly separating these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses adequate retraction to clearly demonstrate the boundaries of the peritoneal sac, ensuring visibility of the anatomical limits during the hernia repair procedure.
After reducing the hernia sac, the surgeon performs preperitoneal dissection extending to the contralateral side, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a retract, buzz, and push technique to separate the thin peritoneum from the posterior sheath, visibly thinning the peritoneal layer during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the difference between adequate and inadequate retraction, showing how proper retraction exposes the surgical field and anatomical structures more clearly.
The surgeon articulates the scissors to bluntly dissect the peritoneum from the posterior sheath, moving in a downward direction to separate the tissue layers.
The hernia sac is visibly reduced and free from the abdominal wall, showing continuity with the dissected peritoneum.
The surgeon uses a metallic instrument to measure the defect size, which is visually confirmed to be just under five centimeters.
The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to ten millimeters of mercury, and the surgeon closes the hernia defect using an O-barb suture in a continuous running fashion.
A 13x15 cm macroporous polypropylene mesh is inserted through an 8 mm trocar and laid flat against the abdominal wall to cover the original defect size.
The surgeon secures the macroporous polypropylene mesh to the abdominal wall using tacks placed at cardinal positions. Subsequently, the mesh is re-peritonealized by applying additional tacks.
